text: The surgeon isolates the superior mesenteric vein, followed by clearing the surrounding tissue to expose the superior mesenteric artery.
The surgeon retracts the pancreatic head and duodenum anteriorly and to the left, exposing the proximal superior mesenteric artery (SMA) for dissection.
The surgeon performs posterior dissection of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) using electrocautery, carefully separating the artery from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon carefully dissects the perineural sheath away from the vessel to optimize oncologic margins, ensuring clear separation between the nerve sheath and the vascular structure.
The surgeon dissects, ligates, and transects the pancreaticoduodenal artery, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon completes the periportal lymph node dissection, mobilizing the surrounding tissue along with the specimen.
The surgeon retracts the specimen inferiorly to begin isolating the portal vein, revealing the anatomical structures surrounding the vein for further dissection.
The surgeon places a vessel loop around the portal vein, visually encircling the vessel to aid in its isolation.
The surgeon creates a graft for venous reconstruction using the left renal vein, preparing it for integration into the surgical site.
The surgeon uses a stapler to divide the insertion of the left renal vein graft into the inferior vena cava (IVC), completing the venous reconstruction.
The surgeon transects the distal stump sharply and oversews it with a running suture using VivoProLean, ensuring secure closure of the transected area.
The surgeon carefully preserves the left gonadal vein to maintain venous drainage of the kidney, ensuring its integrity during the procedure.
The surgeon identifies the inferior mesenteric vein, ligates it, and transects it. Subsequently, the splenic vein is similarly ligated and transected.
The surgeon performs the final dissection of the anterior attachments to the superior mesenteric artery, carefully separating these structures from the artery.
The specimen remains attached solely by the portal vein and superior mesenteric vein, with other vascular connections already transected.
The surgeon preserves the left gastric vein, ensuring venous drainage of the stomach is maintained.
The surgeon places a clamp on the superior mesenteric vein, preparing for further manipulation or transection of this vessel.
The surgeon places a clamp on the portal vein and proceeds to transect it, completing the separation of the specimen from the portal vein.
The surgeon completes the separation of the specimen by detaching its final attachment to the superior mesenteric vein, fully freeing the specimen from surrounding structures.
The specimen is placed in the left upper quadrant, and the surgeon begins immediate reconstruction to minimize the time the clamps are applied.
The surgeon positions the renal vein graft and begins the proximal anastomosis using a running 5-0 Prolene suture, ensuring precise alignment and secure attachment.
The surgeon clamps the superior mesenteric artery during the prolonged reconstruction phase to minimize balladema.
The surgeon completes the distal anastomosis between the portal vein and the renal graft using a running 5-0 Prolene suture, ensuring secure vascular connection.
The surgeon performs routine intraoperative ultrasound to assess the patency and flow of the renal vein graft, visually confirming the presence of blood flow through the graft.
The surgeon sequentially removes the clamps, starting with the distal clamp, followed by the proximal clamp, and finally the superior mesenteric artery clamp, after completing the anastomosis.
